gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to design peptide–drug conjugate ligands targeting the kappa-opioid receptor (KOR) to achieve high affinity, selectivity, and biased signaling (G protein over β-arrestin) with reduced side effects. The approach combines computational design using Rosetta, chemical synthesis, pharmacological profiling, in vivo testing, and cryo-EM structural validation.

section_gists.heading: Computational design of KOR-targeted peptide-small molecule conjugates
section_gists.gist: Developed a computational design approach using Rosetta to create cyclic peptide–small molecule conjugates targeting KOR. Started from MP1104 derivative bound to KOR, modeled dipeptide extensions, sampled backbone torsions, and grafted thioether cyclized peptides. Iterative sequence design optimized interactions, filtered by binding energy, shape complementarity, and interface area. Selected six hexamer conjugates for synthesis.
process_signals: Hypothesis: small molecule provides affinity, cyclic peptide modulates selectivity and efficacy via ECL interactions.
Design constraints: lariat peptides cyclized via sidechain and N-terminus, C-terminus available for conjugation; 5-6 residue macrocycle.
Filtering steps: 90th percentile cut-off on ΔΔG, shape complementarity, interface area; further filtered by ECL2/3 interactions and diversity.
methods: Rosetta peptide design methods (FastDesign, backbone generation, clustering)

section_gists.heading: Chemical synthesis of the designed ligands and determination of their pharmacodynamic properties at KOR
section_gists.gist: Synthesized linear (DNLP) and cyclic (DNCP) peptides; none displaced [3H]DPN at 10 μM, indicating no standalone binding. Synthesized peptide–drug conjugates using β-NalA instead of MP1104 derivative for simpler synthesis and flexibility. Conjugates DNCP-β-NalA(1–6) showed varying binding affinities; DNCP-β-NalA(1) had highest affinity (Ki = 14 nM). Functional assays (cAMP) showed DNCP-β-NalA(1) is a full agonist (EC50 = 8.5 nM) with higher potency than β-NalA alone. Mutagenesis identified key residues for binding.
process_signals: Failure: standalone peptides (linear and cyclic) did not bind KOR, validating conjugate strategy.
Design revision: used β-NalA instead of MP1104 derivative due to simpler synthesis and flexibility.
Success: DNCP-β-NalA(1) showed nanomolar affinity and full agonism.
Partial results: other conjugates had lower affinity; DNCP-β-NalA(1) selected for further study.
failures_or_limits: Linear and cyclic peptides alone did not bind KOR.
Other conjugates (2–6) had lower affinity than DNCP-β-NalA(1).

section_gists.heading: Pharmacological profiling of DNCP-β-NalA(1) for receptor subtype selectivity and functional bias
section_gists.gist: DNCP-β-NalA(1) fully activated human KOR (EC50 = 5.5 nM, Emax = 83%) in [35S]GTPγS assay. Showed ~80-fold selectivity over DOR, ~330-fold over NOP, but equipotent at MOR. In β-arrestin recruitment, DNCP-β-NalA(1) was a partial agonist (Emax = 25%) with bias factor ~30 for G protein over β-arrestin. Mutagenesis revealed ECL2/3 and TM6/7 residues modulate bias.
process_signals: Key finding: biased agonism (G protein > β-arrestin) may reduce side effects.
Limitation: equipotent at MOR, but acts as antagonist at MOR, potentially contributing to favorable profile.
Structure-function insights from mutagenesis guided understanding of bias.
failures_or_limits: No selectivity over MOR; mixed KOR agonist/MOR antagonist profile.

section_gists.heading: Antinociceptive efficacy and lack of typical KOR-mediated side effects of designed peptide–small molecule conjugate
section_gists.gist: DNCP-β-NalA(1) showed serum stability (>95% after 48 h). In formalin test, dose-dependent antinociception (ED50 = 1.89 μmol/kg), equipotent to U50,488, reversed by nor-BNI. In CFA inflammatory hyperalgesia, fast onset (peak 15 min) but short duration (return to baseline by 2 h). Also reduced paw edema (anti-inflammatory), unlike U50,488. No motor impairment in rotarod test at effective doses, unlike U50,488.
process_signals: Success: in vivo efficacy with reduced side effects (no sedation/motor dysfunction).
Partial result: shorter duration than U50,488.
Unexpected: anti-inflammatory effect not seen with U50,488.

section_gists.heading: Structural validation of peptide-small molecule design
section_gists.gist: Cryo-EM structure of KOR–DNCP-β-NalA(1)–Gi1 at 2.6 Å. Small molecule occupies orthosteric site; peptide binds extracellular pocket formed by ECL2/3, TM6/7. Design model partially validated: D-Phe position conserved, but D-Tyr interacts with ECL3 instead of predicted ECL2. MD simulations showed stable peptide interactions. Mutations E209A, E297A, L309A increased β-arrestin bias and Gi potency.
process_signals: Validation: overall binding mode as designed, but specific ECL interactions differed.
Insight: identified residues controlling bias, explaining pharmacological profile.
Limitation: only one structure; other conjugates not structurally characterized.
failures_or_limits: Predicted ECL2 interaction not observed; D-Tyr interacts with ECL3 instead.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Designed conjugates mimic MP1104 orthosteric properties and engage secondary binding sites. Only four compounds synthesized to find high-affinity biased agonist. DNCP-β-NalA(1) is full agonist with G protein bias, ~80-fold selective over DOR, but equipotent at MOR (acts as antagonist). Mixed KOR agonist/MOR antagonist may contribute to favorable side effect profile. Cryo-EM revealed ECL2/3 and TM6/7 network controlling bias. Mutations increased β-arrestin bias, suggesting designability of bias. Limitations: MOR activity, short in vivo duration, need for further optimization.
process_signals: Efficiency: small library yielded potent compound.
Mixed MOR activity may be beneficial but needs optimization.
Structural basis for bias identified, enabling future design.
Acknowledged limitations: MOR equipotency, short duration, only male mice.
failures_or_limits: Need for further optimization of pharmacokinetics and selectivity.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from computational design of peptide–drug conjugates to experimental validation, with iterative refinement based on binding and functional data.
Initial hypothesis that cyclic peptide alone could bind was disproven, reinforcing the conjugate strategy.
Switch from MP1104 derivative to β-NalA was a practical decision that maintained activity.
Biased signaling and mixed KOR/MOR pharmacology were serendipitous but explained by structure.
Structural validation confirmed overall design but revealed unexpected interaction details, guiding future optimization.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How to improve MOR selectivity while retaining KOR affinity and bias?
Can the short in vivo duration be extended through chemical modifications?
Would other conjugates from the designed library show different bias or selectivity?
Are the findings translatable to female mice and other pain models?
Can the anti-inflammatory effect be separated from antinociception?

events.summary: The development of efficient technologies for rational design of high-affinity peptide ligands for single GPCRs remains an unmet challenge.
Hypothesis that combining small molecule and peptide advantages via computational design of peptide–small molecule conjugates can yield ligands with unique pharmacological properties.
Design constraints: lariat peptides cyclized via sidechain and N-terminus, C-terminus available for conjugation; 5-6 residue macrocycle.
Computational modeling of dipeptide extensions and backbone sampling to select four dipeptide solutions with lowest Rosetta binding energy.
Generation of thioether cyclized peptide library and iterative sequence design using Rosetta FastDesign, filtered by binding energy, shape complementarity, and interface area.
Six thioether cyclized hexamer conjugates selected for synthesis based on ECL2/3 interactions and diversity.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Design of peptide–drug conjugate ligands targeting the kappa-opioid receptor (KOR) to achieve high affinity, selectivity, and biased signaling (G protein over β-arrestin) with reduced side effects.

canonical_events.summary: Rational design of high-affinity peptide ligands for single GPCRs remains an unmet challenge.
Hypothesis that combining small molecule and peptide advantages via computational design of peptide–small molecule conjugates can yield ligands with unique pharmacological properties.
Design constraints: lariat peptides cyclized via sidechain and N-terminus, C-terminus available for conjugation; 5-6 residue macrocycle.
Computational design workflow using Rosetta: dipeptide extension, backbone generation, docking, and filtering by binding energy, shape complementarity, and interface area.
Six thioether cyclized hexamer conjugates selected for synthesis based on ECL2/3 interactions and diversity.
Design revision: used β-NalA instead of MP1104 derivative for simpler synthesis and flexibility.
Pharmacological profiling: DNCP-β-NalA(1) is a full agonist in [35S]GTPγS assay (EC50 = 5.5 nM, Emax = 83%), ~80-fold selective over DOR, ~330-fold over NOP, but equipotent at MOR.
β-arrestin-2 recruitment assay: DNCP-β-NalA(1) is a partial agonist (Emax = 25%) with bias factor ~30 for G protein over β-arrestin.
In vivo antinociception: DNCP-β-NalA(1) dose-dependently reduced pain in formalin test (ED50 = 1.89 μmol/kg), equipotent to U50,488, reversed by nor-BNI.
In vivo side effect profile: DNCP-β-NalA(1) showed no motor impairment in rotarod test, unlike U50,488, and reduced paw edema (anti-inflammatory effect).
Cryo-EM structure of KOR–DNCP-β-NalA(1)–Gi1 at 2.6 Å validated overall binding mode but revealed D-Tyr interacts with ECL3 instead of predicted ECL2.
Limitations: equipotent MOR activity, short in vivo duration, only tested in male mice, need for further optimization.

source.title: Design and structural validation of peptide–drug conjugate ligands of the kappa-opioid receptor

initial_request.research_problem: Rational design of high-affinity peptide ligands for single GPCRs remains an unmet challenge.
initial_request.known_context: Small molecule ligands penetrate deeply into GPCR binding clefts; peptides make extensive target interactions for specificity.
constraints: Achieve high affinity and selectivity for KOR

quantifiable_goals: In vivo antinociception with reduced side effects
